briefs.title: Council to consider new rules for determining tenant eligibility dates under Burnaby's TAP
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on a set of criteria that staff would use to decide whether tenants keep their original eligibility date — the date that determines who qualifies for moving help and compensation — when a developer resubmits a rezoning application. Under the proposed framework, tenants would keep their original eligibility date only if a tenant meeting was held and forms were distributed, AND all later tenants signed a written acknowledgement that they are not eligible for benefits.
briefs.action_required: If you own rental property in Burnaby with a rezoning application in progress, review whether your tenants have signed the required TAP addendum.
briefs.industries: Landlord & Property Mgmt, Tenant, Construction & Development

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to update how advisory body rules are created and changed
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a bylaw amendment that would change how 'Terms of Reference' — the rulebooks that guide Burnaby's advisory committees — are created and updated. Under the proposed change, the Mayor would direct the preparation of these documents, which would then go to the Executive Committee of Council for review before full Council approves them.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the final adoption vote — this affects how Burnaby advisory bodies operate.

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to Cut Plumbing Permit Fees and Offer Refunds for Some Developments
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a bylaw that would temporarily reduce plumbing permit fees for most developments (excluding small-scale multi-unit housing and tenant improvements). Under the proposal, the fee for a first fixture would drop from $121 to $70, and each additional fixture from $67 to $39, for permits issued on or before June 30, 2026. Eligible permit holders who already paid the higher fees between January 1 and March 10, 2026 could receive a refund for the difference.
briefs.action_required: If you pulled a plumbing permit in Burnaby after Jan 1, 2026, check if you qualify for a refund once the bylaw passes.

key_dates.label: Burnaby reduced plumbing permit fee window closes — permits must be issued by this date

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Bylaw Would Set $500 Fines for Bear Spray Sales Violations
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw amendment that would add $500 fines for businesses that break rules around selling bear spray. Under the proposal, retailers could be fined for selling to anyone under 19, failing to check photo ID, improper storage, or not keeping proper sales records — with no discount or reduced-penalty option available for any of these violations.
briefs.action_required: If you sell bear spray, review your storage, ID-checking, and record-keeping practices before this bylaw is adopted.

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Bylaw Would Restrict Who Can Sell Bear Spray
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would limit bear spray sales in Burnaby to outdoor and sporting goods retailers only — no general retailers or convenience stores. If approved, these authorized sellers would need to check ID, refuse sales to anyone under 19, lock up their stock, and keep detailed sales records for at least 12 months.
briefs.action_required: If you sell bear spray, check whether your business qualifies as an 'authorized vendor' under the proposed rules.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Bear Spray Bylaw — council adoption vote (final approval)

briefs.title: Council to vote on final approval of mid-rise rental building at 5889 Barker Avenue, Burnaby
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on the final adoption of a rezoning bylaw (Bylaw 14782, Amendment No. 19) that would allow a mid-rise rental apartment building at 5889 Barker Avenue in the Metrotown area. The project, proposed by Concord Greenhouse (Nominee) Ltd., would include 106 rental units — 48 of which would be non-market (below-market-rate) rentals with rules on how much rent can be charged and who qualifies to live there.
briefs.action_required: If this development affects your business or property nearby, attend the March 10, 2026 Council meeting to have your say.

key_dates.label: Burnaby REZ 25-15 rezoning bylaw — Council final adoption vote

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Tenant

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to vote on rezoning amendment for Barker + Olive development
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw (No. 14782) that would amend Burnaby's zoning map to update the rules for a specific piece of land tied to the 'Barker + Olive' development project. The change would bring the site in line with a revised development plan prepared by IBI Group, affecting how that land can be built on and used.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council adoption vote in early 2026 — this could affect nearby property or development plans.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Zoning Bylaw Amendment No. 19, 2025 — council final adoption vote

briefs.title: Council to vote on final approval of six-storey self-storage facility at Still Creek Avenue
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on the final adoption of a rezoning bylaw (Bylaw 14763) that would allow a six-storey self-storage building with underground parking to be built at 3942–3944 Still Creek Avenue. The applicant has met all required conditions set by the City, and the Planning Department is recommending Council give the bylaw its final approval at the March 10, 2026 meeting.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the Council decision on March 10, 2026 — this rezoning could affect nearby businesses.

key_dates.label: Burnaby REZ 25-05 rezoning bylaw final adoption vote — Council meeting

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Transportation & Logistics

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to vote on rezoning for self-storage facility at Still Creek
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would rezone two properties at 3942 and 3944 Still Creek Avenue in Burnaby to allow for an amended self-storage development. The rezoning — a change to the rules about how land can be used — would update an existing Comprehensive Development Plan (a detailed blueprint for how the site is built and used) to reflect new plans prepared by Studio One Architecture Inc.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the final council adoption vote in 2026 — this could affect nearby property use and development.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Zoning Bylaw Amendment No. 14, 2025 — final adoption vote

briefs.title: Council to vote on final approval of rental tower and community hub in Edmonds
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on the final adoption of a rezoning bylaw (Bylaw 14744) that would allow a residential rental tower with a community hub to be built at 7135 Walker Ave. and 7244 Arcola St. in the Edmonds neighbourhood. The applicant, Gracorp Properties LP, has met all the conditions set by the City, and Council will consider giving the bylaw its final sign-off at the March 10, 2026 meeting.
briefs.action_required: Watch the March 10, 2026 Council meeting if this development affects your business or property.

key_dates.label: Burnaby REZ 24-20 rezoning bylaw final adoption vote — Council meeting

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Tenant

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Rezoning of Two Properties on Walker and Arcola Streets
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw (Bylaw No. 14744) that would rezone two properties — 7135 Walker Street and 7244 Arcola Street — in Burnaby. If approved, the rezoning would change what can be built or used on those lands, and any future development would have to follow new guidelines attached to the bylaw.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the final council adoption vote — this rezoning could affect nearby property use and development plans.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Zoning Bylaw No. 14744 — final adoption vote scheduled

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Zoning Bylaw Amendment to Create New Employment, Industrial, and Agricultural Districts
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on Bylaw No. 14792, which would update Burnaby's zoning rules by creating several new land-use zones — including an Employment District (E1), Industrial District (I1), Interim Parks/Open Spaces District (PM1), Interim Institutional District (PM2), and Interim Agricultural District (AM1). The proposed bylaw would also add dozens of new definitions (such as 'light industrial,' 'commissary kitchen,' and 'works yard') and update rules around building height, fencing, parking, home-based businesses, and short-term rentals across many existing zones.
briefs.action_required: Check if your property or business is in an affected zone — this could change what uses are permitted on your lot.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Zoning Bylaw Amendment No. 2, 2026 — third reading scheduled

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Agriculture, Landlord & Property Mgmt

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to vote on energy-based fees for public EV chargers
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on switching Burnaby's public EV charging stations from time-based fees (paying by the hour) to energy-based fees (paying by the kilowatt-hour of electricity actually used). The proposed rates are $0.2865/kWh for Level 2 chargers and $0.3479/kWh for Level 3 chargers, plus idle fees if a fully charged vehicle stays plugged in during peak hours.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — new EV charging rates could affect your business fleet or employee parking costs.

key_dates.label: Burnaby energy-based EV charging fees — 30-day public notice period begins after Council approval

briefs.title: Proposed: New Fine for Exceeding Time Limit at EV Charging Parking Spaces in Burnaby
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a bylaw amendment that would add a new fine for drivers who stay too long in an electric vehicle (EV) metered parking space. Under the proposal, overstaying the maximum time limit at an EV charging meter would result in a $75 fine (reduced to $60 if paid early).
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — this could affect staff or customers who use EV charging spaces near your business.

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to update EV charging fees at city-owned parking stations
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a bylaw amendment that would replace the current electric vehicle (EV) charging fee structure at city-owned stations with two new rate tables. One table sets energy-based rates (charged per kilowatt-hour of electricity used), and the other sets time-based rates (charged by how long you park). Idle fees — extra charges for leaving your car plugged in after charging is done — would also apply between 6 a.m. and 10 p.m.
briefs.action_required: Check if your business relies on city EV charging stations — new rates could affect your operating costs.

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to update EV charging meter rules and fee structures
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a bylaw amendment that would update how electric vehicle (EV) charging meters are defined and how drivers are charged for using them. The proposed changes would allow fees to be based on either time (how long you park) or energy used (how much electricity you draw), and would set new limits on how long a vehicle can occupy an EV charging space.
briefs.action_required: If you operate EV charging or parking in Burnaby, watch for the council vote and review the updated fee structure.
briefs.industries: Transportation & Logistics, Retail, Landlord & Property Mgmt

briefs.title: Council to consider Burnaby RCMP quarterly crime report for July–September 2025
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to review the Burnaby RCMP's performance report covering July to September 2025. The report shows mixed results for local businesses: break-ins at commercial properties dropped 13%, fraud fell 19%, and auto theft was down 31%, but theft from vehicles jumped 45%, robberies rose 27%, and drug trafficking offences increased 123% compared to the same period last year.
briefs.action_required: Review the theft-from-vehicle and break-and-enter trends — consider upgrading your business security now.

briefs.title: Council to consider Burnaby RCMP Public Safety Report for August–November 2025
briefs.summary: Council is set to review the Burnaby RCMP Public Safety Report covering August to November 2025. The report outlines policing activity across the city during that period, including drug seizures, robbery investigations, traffic enforcement, gang suppression operations, and community outreach — giving Council a picture of how public safety resources were used.
briefs.action_required: If your business was affected by crime in this period, review the report for relevant updates.

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Burnaby RCMP Policing Status Report for August–November 2025
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to receive an information report from the RCMP Officer in Charge covering policing operations from August to November 2025, along with a quarterly performance update for July to September 2025. No new rules or bylaws are being voted on — Council will simply review crime statistics, policing highlights, and community safety initiatives, including the online crime reporting tool that handles about 200 reports per period.
briefs.action_required: No action required — this is an information-only report with no new rules or decisions affecting businesses.

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Burnaby Fire Department Status Report for End of 2025
briefs.summary: Burnaby's Public Safety Committee is set to review the Burnaby Fire Department's status report covering activity through December 2025. The report covers major incidents, call volume trends, fire prevention inspections, training hours, and community outreach — giving Council a snapshot of how fire services are being used and where demand is growing.
briefs.action_required: If your business was inspected or flagged, watch for any follow-up from Burnaby Fire Prevention.
briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Food Service, Landlord & Property Mgmt

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Burnaby Fire Department Status Update for December 2025
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to receive a status update report from the Burnaby Fire Department covering activities through the end of December 2025. The report is informational only — no new rules or requirements are being proposed — and covers emergency response, fire prevention, inspections, and community safety education.

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Survey Results from Burnaby's Online Child Safety Workshop
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to review the results of a November 2025 workshop on combatting online sexual exploitation of children. The survey shows strong attendee satisfaction and highlights requests for future sessions on topics like navigating the justice system, talking to parents, and protecting neuro-diverse youth online.
briefs.action_required: Review the survey findings if your business works with youth, families, or social services in Burnaby.

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Status Update on Burnaby–New Westminster Youth Exploitation Task Force
briefs.summary: Council is set to receive a status update on the Burnaby–New Westminster Task Force on Sexually Exploited and At-Risk Youth. The report covers the Task Force's recent activities — including information sharing, a professional development day on online sexual exploitation, and plans for future workshops in 2026. No new funding or bylaw changes are being proposed at this time.

briefs.title: Council to Consider Updates on Major Burnaby Recreation and Civic Building Projects
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to receive status updates on seven major civic construction projects, including a new RCMP building, three new recreation centres, and a theatre renewal. Council will also consider a staffing and operations plan — called People-Play — to prepare for opening the new recreation facilities, which will nearly double the city's recreation services by 2030.
briefs.action_required: Watch for Council decisions on facility openings and budgets — these may affect local hiring, contracts, and services near your business.

key_dates.label: Mintara Restaurant patio expansion — project handover to Parks, Recreation and Culture
Burnaby People-Play phased launch scenarios — presented to Council for budget direction
James Cowan Theatre renewal — project handover anticipated
Community Safety Building (RCMP detachment) — construction completion anticipated
Burnaby Lake Recreation Centre — project handover anticipated
Cameron Community Centre and Library — project handover anticipated
Brentwood Community Centre — construction completion anticipated

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Labour & Employment, Food Service

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Burnaby's 2025 Gaming Reserve Spending Plan
briefs.summary: Council will consider approving Burnaby's 2025 Gaming Reserve budget, which outlines how casino grant money is spent across the city. The plan shows an opening balance of about $10.8M, roughly $13.6M in new funds added, and over $12.7M in planned spending on community programs, city events, environmental work, and infrastructure — leaving a projected year-end balance of about $11.7M.
briefs.action_required: Review the spending priorities to see if any funded programs affect your business or neighbourhood.

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Burnaby Gaming Reserve Financial Summary 2000–2025
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to review a financial summary of its Gaming Reserve — a special fund built from casino revenue — covering the years 2000 to 2025. The report shows the reserve received nearly $239M in casino grants and earned investment income over that period, while spending about $259M on capital and operating projects, leaving a balance of roughly $11.7M at the end of 2025.
briefs.action_required: Monitor how Council allocates Gaming Reserve funds — future spending could affect local infrastructure and services near your business.
briefs.industries: Finance & Insurance, Construction & Development, Professional Services

briefs.title: Council to consider: Status update on Burnaby's 2025 Casino Gaming Reserve spending
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to receive a status update on the Gaming Reserve — a fund built from casino revenue shared with the city by the Province of BC. In 2025, the city spent about $12.7 million from this reserve on programs and projects, and the reserve held roughly $11.7 million at year-end.
briefs.action_required: No action required — this is an information-only report with no direct impact on business owners.

briefs.title: Council to consider: 2025 Burnaby Mountain Safety Fund spending report
briefs.summary: Council will consider a report showing how $6.68 million from the Burnaby Mountain Community Contribution Fund — a reserve set aside for safety projects on Burnaby Mountain — was spent in 2025. The money went toward three projects: a new emergency access route on the mountain, a vehicle to detect toxic airborne chemicals near industrial sites, and operating costs for Fire Station 8.
briefs.action_required: If your business operates near Burnaby Mountain, review the safety projects — they may affect access routes and emergency response times.
briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Transportation & Logistics, Natural Resources

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Status Update on Burnaby Mountain Safety Reserve Funded by Trans Mountain
briefs.summary: Council is set to receive a status update on how Burnaby is spending $20.1 million from Trans Mountain Corporation over 21 years. The money is earmarked for safety projects on Burnaby Mountain, including a new fire station, a hazardous materials response vehicle, an alternate evacuation route for SFU, emergency exercises, safer firefighter gear, and early wildfire detection.
briefs.action_required: Watch for council updates if your business operates near Burnaby Mountain — new safety infrastructure may affect your area.
briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Transportation & Logistics, Natural Resources

briefs.title: Council to Consider Supporting Tourism Burnaby's Bid to Host 2028 Urban Forest Conference
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on whether to support Tourism Burnaby's plan to submit a bid to host the 2028 Canadian Urban Forest Conference. This is a 'support in principle' only — meaning no city money is being committed yet — but it would allow Tourism Burnaby to move forward with a proposal before the May deadline.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — a successful bid could bring ~350 conference delegates to Burnaby in 2028.

key_dates.label: Tourism Burnaby — 2028 CUFC bid submission deadline

briefs.industries: Hotel & Accommodation, Food Service, Transportation & Logistics

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to Consider 25-Year Parks, Recreation and Culture Master Plan
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on 'Making Life Better,' a draft 25-year plan that would guide how Burnaby spends money on parks, recreation centres, pools, arenas, trails, and cultural programs through 2050. The proposed plan sets service-level targets for 15 areas and outlines 25 actions — ranging from upgrading aging facilities to expanding public art and trails — to be phased in over short, medium, and long terms.
briefs.action_required: Review the draft plan and submit feedback to Burnaby Council before it is approved.

briefs.title: Council to vote on Burnaby's new long-range Parks, Recreation and Culture Plan
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on approving a draft plan called 'Making Life Better: Parks, Recreation and Culture Plan' — a long-range roadmap for parks, rec centres, and cultural services across the city. If approved, staff would also be authorized to launch a second round of public engagement this spring, where residents can review and give feedback on the draft before a final version goes to Council for adoption in late spring 2026.
briefs.action_required: Attend a public drop-in or complete the online survey this spring to share your feedback on the draft plan.

key_dates.label: Burnaby PRC Plan — Council vote on draft approval and public engagement launch
Burnaby PRC Plan — second round public engagement opens
Burnaby PRC Plan — final plan expected to go to Council for approval

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to Consider Updated Community Grant Application Form for 2026
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to consider a revised Community Grant Application form that non-profit and community organizations would use to apply for city funding in 2026. The updated form outlines the rules applicants must follow — including how grant money can be spent, what financial documents must be submitted, and what happens if funds are not used as promised.
briefs.action_required: If your organization receives Burnaby community grants, review the revised application form before submitting your next application.

briefs.title: Council to Consider Updated Community Grant Policy for Burnaby Non-Profits
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a revised Community Grant Policy that would set the rules for how non-profit organizations apply for City funding. The proposed policy would outline three grant types — operating grants (for day-to-day costs), initiative grants (for specific projects or events), and recreational sports grants (for travel to championships) — along with eligibility rules, funding caps, and reporting requirements.
briefs.action_required: If your business supports or partners with a non-profit, check the new eligibility rules before the April application window opens.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Community Grant Policy — April application window opens
Burnaby Community Grant Policy — October application window opens

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to vote on Community Grant Policy and Application updates
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on changes to its Community Grant Policy and application form. The proposed updates would clarify what grant money can and cannot be used for, require non-profits to have a Burnaby address, remove one-time events from Food Security grant eligibility, and keep the current equal-split funding method when grant requests exceed available funds.
briefs.action_required: If you run a non-profit in Burnaby, review the proposed grant policy changes before they take effect.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Community Grant Policy amendments — Council vote date

briefs.title: Council to vote on: Renewing Burnaby's IT ticketing software licences for ~$581K
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on awarding a contract to ServiceNow, Inc. worth an estimated $580,985 (including taxes) to renew software licences for the City's internal IT ticketing system from March 16, 2026 to March 15, 2029. This is an internal City technology contract and is unlikely to directly affect most small businesses.
briefs.action_required: No action required — this is an internal City IT contract with no direct impact on small businesses.

key_dates.label: Burnaby ServiceNow IT ticketing licence contract start date

briefs.title: Council to Consider Applying for Up to $200,000 in Provincial Development Approvals Grant
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on whether to apply for up to $200,000 in provincial grant funding through the UBCM Local Government Development Approvals Program (LGDAP). If approved, the money would help the City speed up building permits and development approvals — especially for small-scale housing, modular construction, and electrical service connections — making it faster and easier to get projects approved.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — faster permit approvals could affect your development or construction timelines.

briefs.title: Proposed: Statutory Right of Way Over Property at 4111 Ledger Avenue, Burnaby
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to consider a proposed statutory right of way — a legal arrangement that gives the City specific access or use rights over a portion of private land — at 4111 Ledger Avenue. Planning sketches show the proposed right of way covers a defined strip of the property, with dimensions roughly 12.5m × 2.8m to 8.1m, and no final decision has been made yet.
briefs.action_required: If you own or operate near 4111 Ledger Avenue, monitor the Council meeting agenda for the vote date.

briefs.title: Council to vote on granting BC Hydro access to Norland Avenue property for electrical work
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on allowing BC Hydro to use part of the city-owned land at 4111 Norland Avenue. This access — called a Statutory Right-of-Way, meaning a legal permission to use someone else's land for a specific purpose — would let BC Hydro install and maintain electrical infrastructure needed to power the new Community Safety Building being built on the site.
briefs.action_required: Monitor the March 10, 2026 council meeting outcome if your business operates near 4111 Norland Avenue.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council vote — BC Hydro right-of-way at 4111 Norland Avenue

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Urges Metro Vancouver to Slow Down Urban Boundary Expansion Changes
briefs.summary: Burnaby's Mayor and Council have written to the Metro Vancouver Board urging them to pause four proposed changes to the Metro 2050 regional growth strategy — the long-term plan that controls where new housing and development can be built across the region. The proposed changes would make it easier to expand the Urban Containment Boundary (UCB) — the line that separates urban development from farmland, green space, and rural areas — which Burnaby argues could raise costs for homebuyers, increase taxes, harm transit, and damage the environment.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the Metro Vancouver Board's decision — it could affect development costs and land use near your business.

key_dates.label: Metro 2050 amendment options comment period closes — Metro Vancouver

briefs.title: Proposed: Metro Vancouver to Consider Loosening Urban Growth Boundary Rules for Surrey, Delta, Langley
briefs.summary: Metro Vancouver is consulting with member cities — including Burnaby — on four proposed changes to Metro 2050, the region's growth plan. The proposals would make it easier to expand the Urban Containment Boundary (the line that separates urban development from rural and agricultural land) in Surrey, Delta, and Langley Township, by lowering the number of votes needed to approve land-use changes and reducing red tape in the amendment process.
briefs.action_required: Submit comments to Metro Vancouver by March 20, 2026 if this affects your business or property.

key_dates.label: Metro 2050 amendment consultation — comment period closes

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Agriculture

briefs.title: Council to vote on sending concerns about proposed Metro 2050 regional growth changes
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on sending a formal letter to the Metro Vancouver Board opposing four proposed changes to Metro 2050 — the regional plan that guides where growth and development happen across the Lower Mainland. The proposed changes would make it easier to expand the Urban Containment Boundary (the line that separates urban development from rural and agricultural land), and would reduce the level of regional agreement needed to approve major land use changes. Burnaby's concerns include higher costs for water, sewer, and transit services, potential increases to Development Cost Charges (fees builders pay to fund infrastructure), and risks to environmental and housing density goals.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the Metro Vancouver Board's April 24 decision — it could affect development costs and land use rules in your area.

key_dates.label: Metro 2050 amendment options comment period closes — Metro Vancouver Board
Metro Vancouver Board considers initiating Metro 2050 amendment process
Metro 2050 amendment 60-day formal comment period opens — affected local governments
Metro Vancouver Board considers final reading of Metro 2050 amendment bylaw

briefs.title: Council to Consider Liquor Licence Application for 102-6462 Kingsway, Burnaby
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to consider a liquor licence application (LLA 25-12) for a business located at Unit 102-6462 Kingsway. A planning sketch prepared in August 2025 identifies the site and surrounding properties, and Council will review whether to support the application before it proceeds through the provincial licensing process.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the Council decision — it could affect liquor licence approval at this Kingsway location.

briefs.title: Council to vote on liquor licence for Solo Karaoke on Kingsway, Burnaby
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on whether to recommend a liquor primary licence — a permit allowing a business to serve alcohol as its main activity — for Solo Karaoke at 102–6462 Kingsway. City staff, the RCMP, and the provincial Liquor and Cannabis Regulation Branch investigated earlier public concerns and found no evidence of illegal liquor service, and are now recommending Council support the application.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the March 10, 2026 Council decision — it could affect liquor licensing in your area.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council vote — Solo Karaoke liquor primary licence recommendation

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Solo Karaoke Ltd. Delegation Request in Burnaby
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to hear a delegation request from Solo Karaoke Ltd., meaning the business has asked for time to speak directly to Council at an upcoming meeting. The details of what Solo Karaoke Ltd. is seeking — such as a permit, licence, or zoning change — are not fully outlined in the agenda item, but Council will consider the request on March 10, 2026.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the Council decision on March 10, 2026 — it could affect similar entertainment businesses in Burnaby.

key_dates.label: Solo Karaoke Ltd. delegation — Burnaby Council meeting date

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council Votes on Bear Spray Sales Rules, Parking Fees, and More
briefs.summary: At its February 24, 2026 meeting, Burnaby City Council voted on several items affecting local businesses, including proposed bylaw amendments that would regulate who can sell bear spray, increase on-street parking fees, introduce energy-based fees for public EV charging stations, and renew the North Road Business Improvement Area for 2026–2031. Several of these measures still require final bylaw adoption before they take full legal effect.
briefs.action_required: Check if your business sells bear spray or operates near North Road — new rules and BIA levies may apply to you soon.

key_dates.label: Burnaby North Road BIA renewal period begins
Burnaby outdoor special events season opens — noise bylaw exemptions active
Burnaby outdoor special events season closes — noise bylaw exemptions end

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to Replace Entire Zoning Bylaw, Effective July 1, 2026
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to consider a full replacement of its Zoning Bylaw — the rulebook that controls what can be built where and how. The proposed new bylaw would introduce clearer rules on building size, parking, landscaping, and tenant protections, and is expected to come into effect on July 1, 2026.
briefs.action_required: Review the proposed Zoning Bylaw changes before the April/May introduction — new rules could affect your property or business.

key_dates.label: Burnaby new Zoning Bylaw introduced to Council — first reading
Burnaby new Zoning Bylaw adoption by Council
Burnaby new Zoning Bylaw takes effect

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Tenant
